content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
November 24,
1993
Robert E. Leidigh
Olson, Hagel,
Fong, Leidigh,
Waters,
and Fishburn
Capitol Bank Center
300 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento,
CA
95814
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1-93-406
Dear Mr. Leidigh:
This is in response to your letter requesting confirmation of
telephone advice requested on behalf of the Senate Rules Committee
pertaining to Section 89001 of the Political Reform Act
(the
“Act”).
1
Since your request does not name a specific public
official on whose behalf you make your request,
we are treating
your request as one for informal assistance.
2
OUESTION
Does Section 89001 prohibit the mailing of the following in a
single mailing:
(1)
An envelope which displays a Senator’s name
and office address on the outside and
(2)
an insert which
reproduces the materials provided by the National Cancer Institute
and which will include the Senator’s name and office address at
the top of each side of the insert,
where one side of the insert
will be in the English language and the other side will be in the
Spanish language.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000-18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1—93—406
Page
2
CONCLUSION
Section 89001 of the Act does not prohibit the mailing where
an exception to the “mass mailing” prohibitions applies
independently to each item under Regulation 18901 (b).
FACTS
The National Cancer Institute prepared materials for
dissemination relating to the early detection of breast cancer.
A Senator wishes to reproduce the materials, verbatim,
in
English and Spanish in order to communicate the information to
segments of the population which otherwise lack the information,
such as Hispanic non—English speaking women.
The text of the National Cancer Institute’s instructions will
be printed verbatim on each side of an insert in an envelope.
One
side will be in English, the reverse side in Spanish.
The
Senator’s name and office address will appear at the top of each
side of the insert in either letterhead or business card format.
There will be no other mention of the Senator’s name inside the
envelope.
The Senator’s name and office address will also appear
on the outside of the envelope in the typical return, address
location.
ANALYSIS
Mass Mailing Prohibition
In June 1988, Proposition 73 amended Section 89001 of the Act
to provide:
“No newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent at
public expense.”
The Commission adopted Regulation 18901 to
clarify which mass mailings were permissible and which were
prohibited under the Act.
Regulation 18901(a)
provides that a
mailing is prohibited only if all of the following apply:
(1)
Any item sent is delivered,
by any means,
to the recipient at his or her residence, place of
employment or business,
or post office box.
For
purposes of this subdivision
(a) (1), the item
delivered to the recipient must be a tangible item,
such as a videotape,
record,
or button,
o±
a
written document.
(2)
The item sent either:
(A)
Features an elected officer
affiliated with the agency which produces
or sends the mailing,
or
(B)
Includes the name, office,
photograph,
or other reference to an
elected officer affiliated with the


Our File No.
1—93—406
Page
3
agency which produces or sends the mail
ing,
and is prepared or sent in
cooperation,
consultation, coordination,
or concert with the elected officer;
(3) (A)
Any of the costs of distribution
is paid for with public moneys; or
(B)
Costs of design, production,
and printing exceeding $50.00 are paid
with public moneys,
and the design,
production, or printing is dane with the
intent of sending the item other than as
permitted by this regulation.
(4)
More than two hundred substantially
similar items are sent,
in a single calendar
month, excluding any item sent in response to
an unsolicited request and any item described
in subdivision (b).
Regulation 18901(a).
A member of the State Senate is a state e1ected.official.
Thus, the State Senator is a public official subject to the
provisions of Section 89001.
(Section 82048.)
Regulation 18901 sets out limited exceptions to the
prohibition on mass mailings.
An agency may include the name of
an elected official in the standard letterhead or logotype of the
agency.
(Regulation 18901(b) (1).)
The regulation permits
reference to an elected officer’s name,
where the name of the
elected official appears only on the standard letterhead or
logotype of the stationery,
forms,
and envelopes of the official’s
agency,
or a roster listing containing the name of all elected
officers, provided they appear in the same type size, typeface,
type color,
and location.
Any such item may not include an
elected officer’s photograph,
signature,
or any other refererce to
the elected officer, except as specifically permitted by another
exception under subsection
(b)
of the regulation.
The letterhead exception is limited to the inclusion of the
official’s name in the letterhead of the agenc’,
and does not
permit the inclusion of the name in the text itself.
(Goldberg
Advice Letter,
No. A-92-474; Martin Advice Letter,
No. A-9l-423;
Murphy Advice Letter,
No.
A-89-088;
Symkowick Advice Letter,
No.
A—89—492.)
Your facts indicate that the materials sent will
only include the Senator’s name and office address at the top of
each side of the insert, either in letterhead or in business card
format.
Thus, it does not appear that the name of the official
will be included in the text of the material,
and the exception
under either subdivision
(b) (1)
or
(b) (11)
of Regulation
18901 would apply.


Our File No.
1—93—406
Page
4
Furthermore, to the extent that a mailing which meets the
“letterhead” exception is a reproduction of normal letterhead, the
applicable exclusion continues to apply.
(Dixon Advice Letter,
No.
A—93—090.)
3
Please note that your facts are distinguishable from a
situation where the text of material “featuring” an official is
reprinted.
We have advised that a city newsletter,
for example,
may not reprint a page from a sample ballot which lists the
candidates in a local election.
(Culver Advice Letter,
No.
A—90—l40.)
The inclusion of an elected officer’s name in that
format would constitute impermissible “featuring” as defined in
subdivision
(c) (2)
of the regulation,
and would not be permitted.
(Chin Advice Letter,
No.
1-90-403.)
Finally,
under the facts you have provided,
we will treat a
document which is printed in the English language on one side and
in the Spanish language on the other side as meeting an exception
under either subdivision
(b) (1)
or
(b) (11)
as separate items.
Presumably, the translated version is provided solely to reach
non—English speaking women in a language which enables them to
understand the communication being sent.
Therefore,
we believe that this interpretation js consistent
with the Commission’s overall attempt to adhere to the prohibition
set forth in Section 89001,
while permitting specific mailings
consistent with the performance of normal governmental functions
which enable officeholders to communicate with all their
constituents.
for example, the staff memorandum to the
Commission,
Issues Relating tc Regulation 18901
(Mass Mailing),
October 26,
1990.)
Thus, to exclude a mass mailing in a language
not understood by the recipient would seem to lead to absurd
results.
However, please note in order to be consistent with the scope
of Section 89001 and with our prior advice which has narrowly
construed exceptions to Regulation 18901,
incorporation of any
exempted item under this regulation must be determined on a case—
by—case basis.
You indicated that the Senator may use a business card in lieu
of letterhead which would be reproduced at the top of the
materials.
Typically,
business cards are paper clipped or stapled
to a document and sent to some interested person.
The cards
typically bear the seal of the State Senate, the Senator’s name,
office addresses,
and telephone numbers.
On the basis of the
facts you have provided,
we do not believe that reproduction of
the business card on the materials sent in lieu of letterhead
renders the exception under Regulation 18901(b) (11)
inapplicable.


Our File No.
1—93—406
Page 5
I trust this letter addresses your concerns.
If you have any
further questions regarding this matter, please feel free to
contact me at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel, Legal Division
‘
copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of l0c per page.

QUESTION: Does Section 89001 prohibit the mailing of the following in a
single mailing:
(1)
An envelope which displays a Senator’s name
and office address on the outside and
(2)
an insert which
reproduces the materials provided by the National Cancer Institute
and which will include the Senator’s name and office address at
the top of each side of the insert,
where one side of the insert
will be in the English language and the other side will be in the
Spanish language.
;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c) (3).)
 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1—93—406

CONCLUSION: Section 89001 of the Act does not prohibit the mailing where
an exception to the “mass mailing” prohibitions applies
independently to each item under Regulation 18901 (b).
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission November 24, 1993 Robert E. Leidigh Olson, Hagel, Fong, Leidigh, Waters, and Fishburn Capitol Bank Center 300 Capitol Mall, Suite 350 Sacramento, CA 95814 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. 1-93-406 Dear Mr. Leidigh: This is in response to your letter requesting confirmation of telephone advice requested on behalf of the Senate Rules Committee pertaining to Section 89001 of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”). 1 Since your request does not name a specific public official on whose behalf you make your request, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance. 2 OUESTION Does Section 89001 prohibit the mailing of the following in a single mailing: (1) An envelope which displays a Senator’s name and office address on the outside and (2) an insert which reproduces the materials provided by the National Cancer Institute and which will include the Senator’s name and office address at the top of each side of the insert, where one side of the insert will be in the English language and the other side will be in the Spanish language. 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections 18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section 18329(c) (3).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 Our File No. 1—93—406 Page 2 CONCLUSION Section 89001 of the Act does not prohibit the mailing where an exception to the “mass mailing” prohibitions applies independently to each item under Regulation 18901 (b). FACTS The National Cancer Institute prepared materials for dissemination relating to the early detection of breast cancer. A Senator wishes to reproduce the materials, verbatim, in English and Spanish in order to communicate the information to segments of the population which otherwise lack the information, such as Hispanic non—English speaking women. The text of the National Cancer Institute’s instructions will be printed verbatim on each side of an insert in an envelope. One side will be in English, the reverse side in Spanish. The Senator’s name and office address will appear at the top of each side of the insert in either letterhead or business card format. There will be no other mention of the Senator’s name inside the envelope. The Senator’s name and office address will also appear on the outside of the envelope in the typical return, address location. ANALYSIS Mass Mailing Prohibition In June 1988, Proposition 73 amended Section 89001 of the Act to provide: “No newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent at public expense.” The Commission adopted Regulation 18901 to clarify which mass mailings were permissible and which were prohibited under the Act. Regulation 18901(a)
